content:
Evaluation of power generation characteristics of reverse electrodialysis using ion exchange membranes with a novel concavo–convex structure Minato Higa a,b, Suzuka Morinaga c, Ryo Ujike a, Hiroki Kawasaki a, Kaito Okamoto a, Yuji Takaoka a, Yu Sugimoto a,b, Mitsuru Higa a,b,* a Graduate School of Sciences and Technology for Innovation, Yamaguchi University, Japanb Blue Energy Center for SGE Technology (BEST), Japanc Faculty of Engineering, Yamaguchi University, 2-16-1 Tokiwadai, Ube, Yamaguchi, 755-8611, Japan

A R T I C L E I N F O Keywords:Salinity gradient energyReverse electrodialysisProfiled ion-exchange membranesFlat sheet membrane

citation.title: Evaluation of power generation characteristics of reverse electrodialysis using ion exchange membranes with a novel concavo–convex structure

content: A B S T R A C T In recent years, salinity gradient energy (SGE), generated by mixing two salt solutions of different concentrations has gained increasing attention as a renewable energy source. Reverse electrodialysis (RED) uses ion exchange membranes to convert SGE into electrical energy. Enhancing RED power output requires reducing electrical resistance in the low-concentration side-flow channels of the RED stack. This can be achieved using profiled (PF) membranes with convex structures formed on the membrane surfaces. In this study, a novel PF membrane with a convex structure, termed ORIGAMI PF membrane, was fabricated from a single flat membrane forming convex structures keeping the membrane thickness unaffected unlike conventional PF membranes. In power generation tests using NaCl solutions with conductivities of 50 mS/cm and 0.3 mS/cm, the stack constructed with this PF membrane demonstrated a power density of 1.44 W/m2, which was 48 % higher than that of the flat membrane stack. In addition, at a linear velocity of 1 cm/s, the PF membrane stack exhibited a 18 % lower pressure drop than the flat membrane stack; hence, net power density of the PF membrane was 1.24 W/m2, and 70 % higher than that of the flat one.

citation.title: Evaluation of power generation characteristics of reverse electrodialysis using ion exchange membranes with a novel concavo–convex structure

content: ower pressure drop than the flat membrane stack; hence, net power density of the PF membrane was 1.24 W/m2, and 70 % higher than that of the flat one. When high-concentration NaCl solutions with conductivities of 95 mS/cm and 185 mS/ cm were used as the high-concentration feed solutions, the PF membrane stack achieved maximum gross power densities of 2.10 W/m2 and 2.54 W/m2, respectively, demonstrating a 48 % and 33 % increase, respectively, compared to the flat membrane stack under the same conditions. These results demonstrate that the developed PF membrane exhibits superior RED power generation performance compared to flat membranes, even under high-salinity conditions.

citation.title: Evaluation of power generation characteristics of reverse electrodialysis using ion exchange membranes with a novel concavo–convex structure

content: 1. Introduction In recent years, the reduction of greenhouse gas emissions has been identified as a key strategy toward addressing global warming, neces­sitating a rapid decline in fossil fuel use and underscoring the increasing significance of renewable energy technologies [1]. Traditional renew­able energy sources such as solar [2], hydropower [3], wind [4], and geothermal energy [5], have become essential components of the global energy transition. However, major renewable technologies, such as solar and wind power, are inherently intermittent, as they are heavily influ­enced by seasonal and weather conditions, resulting in low and unstable capacity factors [4,6]. The development of more stable renewable en­ergy technologies to complement these sources is crucial. Hence, the salinity gradient energy (SGE) generated when two salt solutions of different concentrations are mixed has attracted increasing interest [7]. Exploiting the salinity difference between seawater and river water at estuarine locations could theoretically provide a global SGE potential of 2.4 TW [8,9], which is comparable to the current global electricity demand. Reverse electrodialysis (RED) is a membrane-based technology for harvesting SGE [7,10–13]. In RED systems, stacks are assembled by alternately layering cation exchange membranes (CEMs) and anion ex­change membranes (AEMs) between two electrodes, with salt solutions on the high salinity (HS) and low salinity (LS) sides supplied to the channels.

citation.title: Evaluation of power generation characteristics of reverse electrodialysis using ion exchange membranes with a novel concavo–convex structure

content: ex­change membranes (AEMs) between two electrodes, with salt solutions on the high salinity (HS) and low salinity (LS) sides supplied to the channels. Electric potentials arise at each membrane–solution interface owing to the selective ion transport of the membranes. Consequently, the total electromotive force increased proportionally with the number of membrane pairs in the stack.

citation.title: Evaluation of power generation characteristics of reverse electrodialysis using ion exchange membranes with a novel concavo–convex structure

citation.title: Evaluation of power generation characteristics of reverse electrodialysis using ion exchange membranes with a novel concavo–convex structure

content: Despite significant advances, several challenges have hindered the commercialization of RED [7,12,14]. These include a low power density per unit membrane area, adverse effects of multivalent ions, scaling and fouling of membranes and electrodes, high capital costs driven mainly by membrane costs, low energy-conversion efficiency, and a limited membrane lifespan. Furthermore, the mass production of large-area ion-exchange membranes (IEMs), essential for RED system scaling, re­mains a substantial challenge. The internal resistance in RED stacks is a major performance limiting factor. Similar to the open-circuit voltage, the internal resistance scales with the number of membrane pairs because the stack resistance is the sum of the electrode resistance and the cumulative cell resistance [11,12]. The overall resistance originates from the CEMs, AEMs, and chan­nels of the HS and LS sides, with the LS channel being the major contributor owing to its inherently lower conductivity. Therefore, reducing the LS channel resistance is crucial for enhancing the RED output [11,12]. Although narrowing the channel spacing between membranes can reduce resistance, it increases the risk of clogging and the overall pumping energy consumption, thereby reducing the system efficiency during prolonged operation [15]. Conventionally, spacer nets are used between the CEMs and AEMs to maintain channel spacing and improve solution mixing [9], as shown in Fig. 1(a).

citation.title: Evaluation of power generation characteristics of reverse electrodialysis using ion exchange membranes with a novel concavo–convex structure

content: 5]. Conventionally, spacer nets are used between the CEMs and AEMs to maintain channel spacing and improve solution mixing [9], as shown in Fig. 1(a). However, these nets are typically made from insulating polymers such as polyethylene (PE) or polypropylene (PP), which block ion diffusion at the membrane surface, leading to increased internal resistance, a phenomenon known as the spacer shadowing effect [16]. In addition, spacer nets cause hy­draulic pressure drops and flow channel clogging because of their hy­drophobicity [17]. To address these limitations, profiled (PF) membranes with embossed surface structures have been proposed [17] (Fig. 1 (b)). PF membranes eliminate the necessity for spacer nets by providing built-in structural support through convex patterns on the membrane surface, maintaining membrane separation, and enhancing mixing [18]. Ver­maas et al. demonstrated a 30 % reduction in the ohmic resistance with PF membranes [17,18], whereas Güler et al. showed that pillar-structured PF membranes outperformed ridge and wave designs in terms of both resistance and net power density, (PDnet) [19]. Pawlowski et al. proposed chevron-shaped PF membranes based on computational fluid dynamics (CFD) simulations [20] and experimentally obtained an 8–14 % higher PDnet with a chevron-type profiled membrane. However, conventional PF membrane designs exhibit certain limitations.

citation.title: Evaluation of power generation characteristics of reverse electrodialysis using ion exchange membranes with a novel concavo–convex structure

content: mentally obtained an 8–14 % higher PDnet with a chevron-type profiled membrane. However, conventional PF membrane designs exhibit certain limitations. By creating localized membrane thickening to form convex structures, they suffer from increased average membrane resistance, deformation owing to swelling inconsistencies, mechanical fragility of unsupported convex areas, and localized concentration polarization and fouling [17–19]. Further studies are required to address these limitations. Herein, we propose a novel PF membrane design inspired by the Japanese art of ORIGAMI (Fig. 1 (c)). We have designated this PF membrane as the “ORIGAMI PF membrane”. Instead of varying the membrane thickness, a uniform concavo–convex structure was created across the flat IEM, maintaining a consistent membrane thickness while generating the necessary three-dimensional geometry. The profiled AEM (PF-AEM) and CEM (PF-CEM) were patterned with convex structures tilted clockwise and counterclockwise, respectively, relative to the di­rection of solution flow. When assembled facing each other along the flow axis in RED cell, this configuration is expected to maintain optimal membrane separation without spacers, minimize contact resistance, suppress concentration polarization, and enhance resistance to fouling, ultimately contributing to higher power densities and lower pressure losses in RED systems. We fabricated and assembled PF membranes with a novel concavo–convex structure.

citation.title: Evaluation of power generation characteristics of reverse electrodialysis using ion exchange membranes with a novel concavo–convex structure

content: ng to higher power densities and lower pressure losses in RED systems. We fabricated and assembled PF membranes with a novel concavo–convex structure. The performances of the two con­figurations were compared by evaluating the power-generation char­acteristics of the RED stacks using PF and flat membrane cells. 2. Experimental 2.1. Fundamental properties of IEMs used in the RED stack The ion exchange membranes (IEMs) used as the AEM and CEM in the RED stack were as follows: Neosepta AMX and Neosepta CMX membranes (Astom Corporation, Japan), which include reinforcement, had a thickness of approximately 140–170 μm. Fumasep FAS-50 and Fumasep FKS-50 membranes (Fumatech GmbH, Germany) had a thickness of 40–70 μm. The IEMs were used as flat membranes in the RED stack. One sheet each of the AMX and CMX membranes was used as the terminal membrane on the electrode side to prevent the leakage of the electrode solution into the model seawater and river water channels. The fundamental properties of the membranes were determined using previously reported methods and equipment [21]. 2.2. Fabrication of PF membranes and measurement of their structure Before preparing the PF membranes, FAS-50 and FKS-50 were immersed in a 0.5 M NaCl solution for a period of 24 h to achieve swelling equilibrium. Subsequently, holes for liquid supply channels were punched into the membranes of the feeding solution using a hy­draulic punching machine (0P-29-60; AKEBONO Machine Industry Co., Ltd., Japan).

citation.title: Evaluation of power generation characteristics of reverse electrodialysis using ion exchange membranes with a novel concavo–convex structure

content: els were punched into the membranes of the feeding solution using a hy­draulic punching machine (0P-29-60; AKEBONO Machine Industry Co., Ltd., Japan). The punched FAS-50 and FKS-50 membranes were placed in a mold and thermally pressed using a high-frequency welder (PLAS­EST-7A; YAMAMOTO VINITA Co., LTD, Japan). The thermal pressing conditions were as follows: anode current of 0.3 A, plate temperature of 70 ◦C, pressing pressure of 0.3 MPa, welding time of 198 s, and cooling time of 99 s. This process formed a concavo–convex mold pattern on the membranes, resulting in the fabrication of PF-AEM and PF-CEM. The concavo–convex structures of the fabricated PF membranes were observed using a confocal laser scanning microscope (OLS5100-EAT, Evident Corporation, Japan). 2.3. The number of the convex parts has been reduced in this diagram to make it easier to see. In reality, there were 53 convex parts spaced approximately 1 mm apart, and the convex heights were approximately 50 μ m (please see Fig. 6). M. Higa et al. Journal of Membrane Science 736 (2025) 124689 where ΔpHS and ΔpLS are the pressure drop at HS and LS channels, QHS and QLS are the flow rates of HS and LS, and η is the pump efficiency (=0.8), respectively. The conductivities of the outlet feed solutions were measured using a conductivity meter (D-220C; HORIBA Advanced Techno Co., Ltd., Japan). A 3.0 mol/dm3 NaCl aqueous solution was used as the electrode solution and was circulated through the system using a magnetic pump (MD-15R–N, IWAKI Co., Ltd., Japan). While circulating these solutions, the load resistance was varied using a custom-built variable resistance device at the values from 0.2 to 200 Ω. The resulting current and voltage were measured using a current–volt­age meter (TDB-401, OHM ELECTRIC Inc., Japan). The obtained data were used to compare the power generation characteristics of the PF membrane stack with those of the flat membrane stack.

citation.title: Evaluation of power generation characteristics of reverse electrodialysis using ion exchange membranes with a novel concavo–convex structure

content: ., Japan). The obtained data were used to compare the power generation characteristics of the PF membrane stack with those of the flat membrane stack. Additionally, there have been reports of using brine from a reverse osmosis seawater desalination plant [22] or saturated brine [23] as high-concentration saltwater for RED, and the power generation characteristics of the PFand flat-membrane stacks were compared for various concentrations of HS feed solutions. These feed solutions included 1.0 M NaCl (95 mS/cm), equivalent to brine discharged from RO seawater desalination plants, and 3.0 M NaCl (185.5 mS/cm), equivalent to concentrated brine. The flow velocity was set to 1.5 cm/s. 3. Results and discussion 3.1. Structure of the fabricated PF membranes The IEMs used in this study are listed in Table 1. PF-AEM and PFCEM were fabricated by embossing FAS-50 and FKS-50 into a con­cavo–convex structure, respectively. The concavo–convex features of the PF membrane were observed using laser microscopy to measure the detailed dimensions of the PF structure. Fig. 5 (a) and 5 (b) show the sample microscopy images ob­tained using PF-CEM. Based on these results, the dimensions of the convex (A) and flat (B) regions were measured, as shown in Fig. 6. As illustrated in Fig. 6, the membrane thickness for both PF-CEM and PFAEM was 54 μ m. The average dimensions of the convex (A) and flat (B) regions were measured as shown in Fig. 6. To closely examine the PF structure, a PF-CEM membrane immersed in an aqueous NaCl solution was placed on a flat table and photographed from an oblique angle, as shown in Fig. 5 (c). The presence of air bubbles within the convex regions indicated that the rear side of the convex structure formed corresponding concave shapes.

3.2. Comparison of power generation characteristics of RED stacks using flat and PF membranes The power-generation characteristics of the RED stacks using flat and PF membranes were compared, as shown in Fig. 7. In both cases, the voltage–current (V–I) curves exhibit an approximately linear relation­ship, which is consistent with theoretical predictions. The open-circuit

citation.title: Evaluation of power generation characteristics of reverse electrodialysis using ion exchange membranes with a novel concavo–convex structure

content: Fig. 7. An example of voltage-current (V–I) and power-current (P–I) curves of (a) the flat membrane stack and (b) the PF membrane stack at the flow rate of 1.0 cm/ s. HS: 50 mS/cm NaCl, LS: 0.3 mS/cm NaCl. The raw data of these figures are shown in Fig. SI-8 and Fig. SI-9. M. Higa et al. Journal of Membrane Science 736 (2025) 124689 voltage (OCV) and internal resistance were determined from the inter­cept and slope of the V–I curves, respectively (Fig.7). The OCV of the flat membrane stack and PF membrane was measured to be 1.2 V, suggesting that the formation of the profiled structure on the membrane surface did not significantly impact its ion-selective perme­ability. In contrast, a notable difference was observed in the internal resistance. While the flat membrane stack exhibited a resistance of 11.3 Ω, the PF membrane stack showed a reduced resistance of 7.85 Ω, which was approximately 30 % lower. This reduction was attributed to several factors: (1) the convex structures on the PF membranes eliminated the need for a spacer net on the LS side, (2) the concentration polarization at the membrane surface was mitigated by the profiled structure, and (3) the effective membrane surface area increased owing to the convex geometry. The maximum power density (PDgross), reached 0.97 W/m2 for the flat membrane stack and 1.44 W/m2 for the PF membrane stack, rep­resenting an improvement of 48 %.

citation.title: Evaluation of power generation characteristics of reverse electrodialysis using ion exchange membranes with a novel concavo–convex structure

content: power density (PDgross), reached 0.97 W/m2 for the flat membrane stack and 1.44 W/m2 for the PF membrane stack, rep­resenting an improvement of 48 %. Additionally, at a linear flow ve­locity of 1.0 cm/s, the average pressure drop was 7.3 kPa for the flat membrane stack and 6.0 kPa for the PF membrane stack, indicating a 18 % reduction. This decrease in pressure drop can also be attributed to the elimination of the spacer net, which reduces fluid friction on the LS side. PDgross of both the flat and PF membrane-based RED stacks increased with increasing linear velocity, and showed almost identical values above a linear velocity of 1.0 cm/s, as similar phenomena have been reported in other papers [17,18]. The pressure drop in the two stacks is shown in Fig. SI-1 in the Supporting Information as a function of the linear velocity. As shown in Fig. SI-2 (a), the pumping energy of the two stacks was calculated using the flow rate and pressure drop, and PDnet was calculated by subtracting the pump energy from PDgross, and shown in SI-2 (b). PDnet of the flat and PF membrane stacks exhibited maximum values of 0.73 and 1.24 W/m2, respectively, at 1.0 cm/s of linear velocity. These findings align well with those of previous studies. Vermaas et al. reported that RED stacks equipped with PF membranes exhibited higher power densities than those using spacer nets at higher Reynolds numbers [18].

citation.title: Evaluation of power generation characteristics of reverse electrodialysis using ion exchange membranes with a novel concavo–convex structure

content: al. reported that RED stacks equipped with PF membranes exhibited higher power densities than those using spacer nets at higher Reynolds numbers [18]. They also demonstrated that replacing spacer nets with PF membranes reduced the total internal resistance (including both ohmic and non-ohmic components) from 50 Ωcm2 to 31 Ωcm2, a reduction of approximately 38 % [17]. In another study, Güler et al. compared various PF membrane geometries and found that columnar PF membranes yielded the lowest ohmic resistance and highest power density [19]. Fig. 8 shows the relationship between linear velocity and maximum power density for RED stacks using flat and PF membranes. The power density of both the flat and PF membrane-based RED stacks increased with increasing linear velocity, and at a linear velocity of 1.0 cm/s, the maximum power density reached saturation and showed little difference compared to the value at 1.5 cm/s, as similar phenomena have been reported in other papers [17,18]. Overall, this study demonstrates that the application of PF mem­branes significantly reduces the internal resistance of RED stacks, thereby enhancing their power output. Furthermore, the reduction in pressure drop contributes to decreased energy consumption for solution pumping, indicating that PF membranes are promising structural modification for the development of highly efficient RED systems. 3.3.

citation.title: Evaluation of power generation characteristics of reverse electrodialysis using ion exchange membranes with a novel concavo–convex structure

content: ion for solution pumping, indicating that PF membranes are promising structural modification for the development of highly efficient RED systems. 3.3. Power generation characteristics of RED stacks using PF membranes with various high-salinity feed solutions In this section, the power generation characteristics of RED stacks using PF membranes were evaluated under two HS conditions: model concentrated seawater (95 mS/cm, 1.0 M NaCl, Fig. 9(a) and (b)) and model concentrated brine (185 mS/cm, 3.0 M NaCl, Fig. 9(c) and (d)), with 0.3 mS/cm NaCl solution as the LS feed. The performance of the PF membrane stacks was compared with that of flat membrane stacks and shown in Fig. SI–3. When the model concentrated seawater was used as the HS feed, OCVs of the flat and PF membrane stacks were measured as 1.28 V and 1.25 V, respectively (Fig. 9(a) and (b)), both of which were lower than the theoretical OCV of 1.55 V, which was calculated from the concen­tration of each side of the stack, assuming that the activity coefficient of NaCl at the solutions is equal to that in the membrane. This discrepancy can be attributed to two factors: (1) a reduction in the transport number of the membranes at high salt concentrations, resulting in increased coion leakage, and (2) osmotic water transport from the LS side to the HS side, which caused a reduction in the concentration gradient across the membrane surfaces due to dilution of the HS side and concentration of the LS side.

citation.title: Evaluation of power generation characteristics of reverse electrodialysis using ion exchange membranes with a novel concavo–convex structure

content: , which caused a reduction in the concentration gradient across the membrane surfaces due to dilution of the HS side and concentration of the LS side. The internal resistance, determined from the slope of the V–I curves, was 7.1 Ω for the flat membrane stack and 4.0 Ω for the PF membrane stack, indicating a 56 % reduction in the PF configuration. Conse­quently, the PF membrane stack achieved a maximum power output of 63 mW, which was 50 % higher than that of the flat membrane stack (42 mW). The corresponding PDgross were 1.42 W/m2 for the flat membrane stack and 2.10 W/m2 for the PF membrane stack, reflecting a 48 % in­crease in power density for the PF configuration. Similarly, when model concentrated brine (185 mS/cm NaCl) was used as the HS feed, the measured OCVs were 1.31 V for the flat mem­brane stack (Fig. 9 (c)) and 1.20 V for the PF membrane stack (Fig. 9 (d)), both lower than the theoretical OCV of 1.77 V. This reduction in the OCV is again attributed to the decreased transport number and con­centration gradient owing to the effect of osmotic water flow. The internal resistance under this condition was 5.49 Ω for the flat membrane stack and 3.51 Ω for the PF membrane stack, showing that the PF membrane reduced the resistance to approximately 64 % of that of the flat membrane. Consequently, the maximum gross power output of the PF membrane stack reached 76 mW, which was 33 % higher than the 57 mW obtained for flat membranes.

citation.title: Evaluation of power generation characteristics of reverse electrodialysis using ion exchange membranes with a novel concavo–convex structure

content: Consequently, the maximum gross power output of the PF membrane stack reached 76 mW, which was 33 % higher than the 57 mW obtained for flat membranes. The corresponding gross power densities were 1.91 W/m2 for the flat membrane stack and 2.54 W/m2 for the PF membrane stack, with the latter demonstrating a 33 % higher power density compared to the case using model seawater. These results indicate that while the OCVs of the PF membranes were comparable to or slightly lower than those of the flat membranes, the internal resistance was consistently reduced to approximately 60 % under the same salinity conditions. This significant reduction in internal resistance enabled the RED stack with the newly developed

Notably, even under high salt concentrations as the HS feed, the PF membrane RED stack demon­strated superior power generation performance.
